ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for ee mee or yi mein noodle dishes; mapped to broader noodle dish class FOODON_03315595.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers cooked pasta and noodles broadly; no specific ee mee or yi mein subcategory exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for ee mee noodle dish; left null to avoid fabrication.

main_ingredients: ee mee noodles (pre-deep-fried alkaline egg noodles)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical hawker serving of seafood ee mee is approximately 400 g, comprising a nest of braised ee mee noodles topped with mixed seafood and vegetables in a savoury gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Seafood Ee Mee — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Seafood Ee Mee (cr_id F05060111). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Asian Noodle Products — Analogous estimate based on egg noodles (cooked), GI ~57–60
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for seafood ee mee in the University of Sydney GI Database. Ee mee noodles are pre-deep-fried alkaline egg noodles; when braised/boiled the GI is estimated at 58 (medium) by analogy with cooked egg noodles (GI 57–60) documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 54.6) / 100 ≈ 31.6, based on 400 g serving with 13.65 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 389 mg per 100 g; a full 400 g serving delivers approximately 1,556 mg sodium, representing 67% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce, avoid consuming the full braising gravy, and limit portion size to no more than 200 g to keep sodium intake below 800 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI of 58 combined with low dietary fibre (0.83 g/100 g) and a high glycemic load of 31.6 per standard serving promotes rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, increase the proportion of seafood and vegetables relative to noodles, and avoid sweet table sauces to reduce glycemic load and postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated trans fat of 0.1 g per 100 g from the pre-deep-fried noodle base and saturated fat of approximately 1.1 g per 100 g contribute to atherogenic lipid risk; sodium at 389 mg per 100 g further elevates cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency, request fresh noodle alternatives to avoid trans fat from the pre-fried ee mee base, and avoid drinking the full gravy to minimise sodium and saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pre-deep-fried ee mee noodles introduce advanced glycation end products (AGEs) and an estimated 0.1 g trans fat per 100 g, both of which are pro-inflammatory and worsen joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid ee mee and substitute fresh noodles such as kway teow or bee hoon, and request fresh fish slices instead of fish cake to reduce processed-meat pro-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seafood Ee Mee (海鲜伊面, Hǎixiān Yī Miàn) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker noodle dish featuring ee mee — a distinctive pre-deep-fried alkaline egg noodle — braised or boiled with mixed seafood (typically prawns, squid, and fish cake) and vegetables (choy sum, bean sprouts) in a savoury cornstarch-thickened gravy seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil. The noodles have a soft, spongy texture that readily absorbs the umami-rich braising liquid, giving the dish its characteristic flavour. Per 100 g, the dish provides 126 kcal, 7.15 g protein, 4.49 g fat, and 13.65 g carbohydrate, with 389 mg sodium and only 0.83 g dietary fibre. The pre-frying of ee mee noodles introduces a medium glycemic index (~58) and an estimated 0.1 g trans fat per 100 g, making the dish a moderate-to-high concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. A standard hawker serving is approximately 400 g, delivering a glycemic load of ~31.6 and approximately 1,556 mg sodium. The dish is a staple at Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops, commonly consumed at lunch or dinner, and is not suitable for vegetarian, vegan, or halal diets without modification.
common_user_queries: How many calories in seafood ee mee?
Is ee mee high in sodium?
Is ee mee suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060111) at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using cooked egg noodles as the reference food. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable egg noodle dishes; trans fat estimated from known pre-frying process of ee mee noodles. Potassium estimated from HPB comparable noodle dish data.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for seafood ee mee is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish as of 2026. cholesterol_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB source for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish entry as of 2026. saturated_fat_g of 1.1 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable cooked egg noodle dishes with seafood (USDA SR Legacy). trans_fat_g of 0.1 g/100 g is estimated from the known pre-deep-frying process used in ee mee noodle manufacture; not directly measured in HPB source. potassium_mg of 140 mg/100 g is estimated from HPB comparable noodle dish data. Input cultural tags (western, vegetarian, plant_based, low_calorie) were incorrect and have been corrected: seafood ee mee is a Singaporean Chinese dish, is not vegetarian or plant-based, and is not classified as low-calorie at 126 kcal/100 g. GI glycemic load per serving recalculated: (58 × 13.65 g/100 g × 400 g serving) / 100 = 31.6.
